rulings.Case Title: TATIANA SANOCHKINA et al VS. MATHEW HUI et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mabel Ten'S 2nd Amended Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Mandatory Dismissal Or, In The Alternative, Discretionary Dismissal Of Pltfs' Complaint, And Mtn To Quash Service Of Summons
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 1: DEFENDANT MABEL TENG'S 2nd Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion For Mandatory Dismissal Or, In The Alternative, Discretionary Dismissal Of Plaintiffs' Complaint, And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons The motion is denied without prejudice. Plaintiff¿s counsel (Mr. Missud) was disqualified from practicing law on July 4, 2013. Further proceedings may not be taken against plaintiffs until defendant provides the notice required by CCP 286. See Aldrich v. San Fernando Valley Lumber Co. (1985) 170 Cal.App.3d 725, 741. Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of defense counsel in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/AJR

rulings.Case Title: CAROL ALFARO VS. MICHAEL ALFARO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Michael Alfaro'S, Macada Inc.'S, Titan Management Group Llc'S, And Aztech Maintenance/Development Llc'S Motion For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 27: Defendants Michael Alfaro's, Macada Inc.'s, Titan Management Group LLC'S, And Aztech Maintenance/Development LLC'S Motion For Summary Adjudication Granted as to Issue nos. 1 ¿ 4, denied as to Issue nos. 6 ¿ 10. The request for judicial notice is granted. For the purposes of this motion only, Defendants assume that Plaintiff is a shareholder of Macada, Inc.. (P&A, p. 1:27-28, fn. 1.) For Issue no. 1, there are no triable issues of fact that Defendant Alfaro did not affirmatively make the misrepresentations alleged in the Complaint. (Complaint, ¶ 62; UMF nos. 5, 6.) To the extent that Plaintiff relies on a subsequent verbal agreement made outside formation of the corporation, these facts fall outside of the pleadings. (FPI Development, Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 380. See AMF nos. 14 ¿ 16.) For Issue no. 2, the fraud claim is time-barred. The doctrines of continuing violation, continuous accrual and equitable estoppel do not toll the statute of limitations in the instant action. (CCP § 338(d); Doe v. Roman Catholic Bishop of Sacramento (2010) 189 Cal.App.4th 1423, 1430.) Plaintiff has not pled tolling of the statute of limitations in the Complaint, and it falls outside the pleadings. It is undisputed that Plaintiff discovered the alleged fraud in 2001 and even provided Defendant Alfaro with semi-monthly statements of the amounts that he purportedly owed. (UMF nos. 11, 12; AMF no. 17.) For Issue no. 3, the fraud claims are not actionable against Defendants Aztech and Titan. Plaintiff's fraud claim is based on the misrepresentations made in 1999 that predate the formation of Defendants Aztech and Titan, and thus, Defendant Alfaro was not acting as their agent when the misrepresentations were made. (See Ach v. Finkelstein (1968) 264 Cal.App.2d 667, 677.) For Issue no. 4, both parties agree that the conspiracy claim is predicated on the fraud claims. For Issue no. 5, Corporations Code § 309 provides a civil right of action. (Corp. Code § 309(c).) Defendants' reliance on Berg & Berg Enterprises, LLC v. Boyle (2009) 178 Cal.App.4th 1020, 1041, Lehman v. Superior Court (2006) 145 Cal.App.4th 109, 123; Briano v. Rubio (1996) 46 Cal.App.4th 1167 are not dispositive as the Court did not address the viability of a cause of action under Corporations Code § 309. For Issue no. 6 ¿ 10, as the only other shareholder in a closely held corporation and Defendant Alfaro's withdrawal of monies to the benefit of himself and to the detriment of Plaintiff in violation of their profit sharing agreement, Plaintiff may assert her claims as an individual, rather than a derivative action, against Defendant Alfaro. Jara v. Suprema Meats, Inc. (2004) 121 Cal.App.4th 1238, 1257-58. See Friedman, Cal. Practice Guide: Corp. (Rutter) ¶ 6:598.2.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party¿s counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/AJR

rulings.Case Title: SHELLEY KRAMER VS. KAUFMAN DOLOWICH VOLUCK & GONZO LLP, A LIMITED et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Further Responses To: Form Interrogatories - Employment- No. 217.1 (Request For Admissions), Set Three: Document Demand, Set Four To Kaufman Dolowich Voluck & Gonzo Llp
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 2, PLAINTIFF SHELLEY KRAMER'S Motion For Further Responses To: Form Interrogatories - Employment- No. 217.1 (Request For Admissions), Set Three: Document Demand, Set Four To Kaufman Dolowich Voluck & Gonzo Llp Pro Tem Judge Robert Kane, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Parties to appear. The Court does not have courtesy copies of the opposition. Defendants shall email electronic courtesy copies of all papers filed in opposition to the motion to rkane1089@aol.com and all other counsel no later than December 16, 2013 at 4 pm. Prior to the hearing, Defendants shall provide physical courtesy copies of these same papers to the courtroom clerk no later than December 17, 2013 at 8:45 am. Any exhibit must be separated by an exhibit tab per CRC 3.1110(f). The court has courtesy copies of the moving and reply papers. =302/JPT

rulings.Case Title: IRENE HSIEH VS. SOLAR POWER INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Solar Power Inc.'S, Stephen Kircher'S, And James Pekarsky'S Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 28: Defendants Solar Power Inc.'s, Stephen Kircher's, and James Pekarsky's Motion For Summary Judgment The Motion for Summary Judgment is denied. The motion for summary adjudication is granted and denied in part. The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the wrongful termination in violation of public policy cause of action. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether plaintiff engaged in protected activity and whether her termination was the result of that activity. The motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the defamation cause of action. Defendant presents declarations from Mr. Kircher and Mr. Pekarsky who clearly state that they never communicated orally or in writing with SolarCity. That evidence shifts the burden of production and in opposition, plaintiff fails to present any conflicting evidence. The Court denies the request for a continuance per CCP 437c(h). First, plaintiff did not formally make such a request in the opposition. Second, plaintiff¿s declarations do not clearly state what facts plaintiff hopes to find to create a triable issue of material fact. Third, plaintiff did not act diligently. The court notes that there was no showing that plaintiff sought to depose witnesses at SolarCity or subpoena documents from that entity. Lastly, a continuance would be prejudicial to defendant given the current trial date. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party¿s counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/AJR

rulings.Case Title: THERABOTANICS, LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY et al VS. SOLAZYME, INC. A DELAWARE CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Therabotanics, Llc'S, A Delaware Limited Liability, And Solabotanics, Llc'S, A Delaware Limited Liability, Motion For Leave To Amend The First Amended Complaint To Add Jonathan Wolfson As A Defendant
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 29: Plaintiffs Therabotanics, LLC's, A Delaware Limited Liability, and Solabotanics, LLC's, A Delaware Limited Liability, Motion For Leave To Amend The First Amended Complaint To Add Jonathan Wolfson As A Defendant Plaintiff¿s motion for leave to amend is granted pursuant to CCP § 473(a)(1). Defendant¿s request for judicial notice is granted. The record does not show that Plaintiffs unreasonably delayed in bringing the present motion. (See Declaration of Aman in Support of Motion, ¶¶8-9, Exhs. 4-5.) Defendants have not shown that they would be prejudiced by allowing the Second Amended Complaint to be filed. Furthermore, Defendants have not shown that the proposed Second Amended Complaint fails as a matter of law, and the defect could not be cured by further appropriate amendment. (See McGuan v. Endovascular Technologies, Inc., (2010) 182 Cal.App.4th 974, 987.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: PAOLI & GEERHART LLP VS. TRAMMELL, ROBERT DBA THE TRAMMELL LAW FIRM et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 16: PLAINTIFF PAOLI & GEERHART LLP'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Part 1 of 2: Granted. All requests for judicial notice are granted. Plaintiff met its burden of producing admissible evidence on each element of the cause of action entitling it to judgment. (CCP § 437c(p)(1).) The elements of breach of contract are: (1) the existence of a contract; (2) plaintiff's performance or excuse for nonperformance; (3) defendant's breach; and (4) damage to plaintiff. (Reichert v. General Ins. Co. (1968) 68 Cal.2d 822, 830.) As to the first element, a contract existed between the parties. (UF #2; Complaint, Ex. A.) As to the second element, plaintiff performed. (UFs #6-8; 14.) As to the third element, defendant breached by not paying 50% of the fees as required by the contract. (UF #19; Complaint, Ex. A.) Plaintiff produced admissible evidence establishing that the case went to mediation as contemplated by the unambiguous contract. (Declaration of Charles Geerhart, Ex. 3, Defendant's response to Form Interrogatory 50.6; Second Declaration of Charles Geerhart, 3-4; Declaration of Tom Paoli, 2-3; Declaration of Elise Vasquez, 2-3.) The Court finds good cause to consider the new evidence submitted with the reply papers because it directly responds to assertions made in Defendant's opposition. (Alliant Ins. Services v. Gaddy (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 1292, 1307-08.) Further, the new evidence was submitted to Defendant prior to the hearing. (Second Decl. of Geerhart, 8.) It is undisputed that Defendant "was the primary author of, and signed, the 15 page letter brief submitted to mediator Williamson dated 7/13/12." (UF # 10.) As to the fourth element, Plaintiff produced evidence showing it was damaged in the amount of $47,587.55 (excluding interest), as it was only paid approximately 23.5% of the earned fees, rather than the 50% set forth in the contract. (UF # 19; Complaint, Ex. A.) Because Plaintiff met its burden of establishing the elements, the burden shifted to Defendant to how that a triable issue of one or more material facts exists as to that cause of action or there is a defense thereto. (Id.) Defendant did not timely submit admissible evidence. Pursuant to CCP § 2015.5, an out-of-state declarant must certify that a declaration is made under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California that the statements are true and correct. The California Supreme Court has upheld strict compliance with this statute. (Kulshrestha v. First Union Comm'l Corp., (2004) 33 Cal.4th 601, 610-611.) The Sworn Declaration of Defendant does not state it is executed under penalty of perjury or under the laws of the State of California. On December 12, 2013, Defendant filed a "Supplemental and Revised Sworn Declaration" that complies with CCP § 2015.5. Even if the Court were to consider this late-filed document, Defendant has not met its burden. Defendant makes conclusory statements about the fact that a mediation did not occur, but does not set forth facts with particularity establishing this. (See e.g. Opposition, p. 5:6-7; Trailmobile, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1989) 210 Cal.App.3d 1451, 56, holding conclusory, vague declarations are "essentially worthless.") Continued to Part 2 of 2. =302/AJR

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES C. CHOW et al VS. TED WIN WONG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 20, DEFENDANT TED WONG, ROGER LOUIE MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Granted in part and denied in part. Granted as to: (1) Page 3, ¶ 8, line 20, the phrase ¿Plaintiffs, other CCBA Board members¿; (2) Page 23, ¶ 95, line 12, the phrase ¿Plaintiffs and¿; (3) Page 23, ¶ 96, line 21, the phrase ¿Plaintiffs and¿; and (4) Page 24, ¶ 101, line 10, the phrase ¿Plaintiffs and.¿ Denied as to the other requests to strike language referring to the Plaintiffs as individuals because the declaratory relief cause of action is being brought by Plaintiffs in their individual capacity. Denied as to the request to strike attorney¿s fees language. If the corporation obtains a monetary recovery from a successful derivative suit, the plaintiff shareholder is entitled to reimbursement for reasonable legal fees and litigation costs incurred in prosecuting the action. (Cziraki v. Thunder Cats, Inc. (2003) 111 Cal.App.4th 552, 557-58.) Denied as to the request to strike the accounting portion of the prayer for relief. The FAC alleges that Defendant Louie had a fiduciary duty to the CCBA and that funds may have been misused in connection with the school renovation project. (FAC, ¶¶ 34, 53; see Kritzer v. Lancaster (1950) 96 Cal.App.2d 1, 6-7.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party¿s counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/AJR

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES C. CHOW et al VS. TED WIN WONG et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 20, DEFENDANT TED WONG, ROGER LOUIE DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT Overruled. As to the first and second causes of action, the First Amended Complaint (¿FAC¿) adequately pleads that the causes of action are being asserted derivatively by members of the CCBA on behalf of the CCBA. (FAC, ¶¶ 94, 99.) Taking everything pled in the FAC as true for purposes of demurrer, as the Court must do, the Business Judgment Rule does not bar these causes of action. The FAC alleges that the defendant directors did not act with good faith, did not act in the best interests of CCBA, and have conflicts of interest. (FAC, ¶¶ 39-40, 50, 52-53, 70, 81.) At the pleadings stage, this is sufficient. As to the third cause of action, there is ¿a justiciable controversy¿ that is ¿definite, concrete, touching the legal relations of parties having adverse interests.¿ (LePage v. City of Oakland (1970) 13 Cal.App.3d 689, 692.) The FAC alleges facts that establish a dispute between members of a corporation on how the bylaws should be properly interpreted and applied. (FAC, ¶ 103.) The uncertainty argument sustained as to the original complaint has been cured by the FAC being brought as a derivative action. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party¿s counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/AJR

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, December 17, 2013, Line 23: APPELLANT INSURANCE COMMISSIONER OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA'S Motion For Approval Of Reinsurance Commutation And Settlement Agreement Grant. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party¿s counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/AJR
